did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper discusses and critiques a study by Haenssle et al. that utilized a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) for dermoscopic melanoma recognition, comparing its diagnostic performance against dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The provided text consists of a 'Letter to the editor' by Oakden-Rayner critiquing a study by Haenssle et al., and a subsequent 'Reply to the letter' by Haenssle et al. While these are original scholarly communications, they are not primary research articles, systematic reviews, or meta-analyses. The document type is a correspondence/letter, which does not fit the strict definitions of 'Research paper' (original experimental study) or 'Review paper' (summarizing prior literature) provided in the instructions. Therefore, the classification is Unknown.

title: Reply to ‘Man against machine: diagnostic performance of a deep learning convolutional neural network for dermoscopic melanoma recognition in comparison to 58 dermatologists’
brief_summary: This correspondence critiques and responds to a study comparing a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) for dermoscopic melanoma recognition against 58 dermatologists. Oakden-Rayner argues that the original study's methodology overestimated the AI's performance by incorrectly equating the ROC area with the area under the curve (AUC) and by selecting an operating point post-experiment to match human sensitivity. In response, Haenssle et al. clarify that the CNN's ROC-AUC (0.864) and the dermatologist's calculated ROC area (0.863) are nearly identical, suggesting the performance comparison was valid despite the metric differences. The authors note that while comparing these metrics is not ideal, alternative methods for estimating AUC from single operating points yield similar results. They conclude that the CNN's diagnostic performance is comparable to that of expert dermatologists.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on 'dermoscopic melanoma recognition' using a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) compared to dermatologists.

all_task_types.details: The paper discusses a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) performing 'dermoscopic melanoma recognition' and compares its diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, ROC-AUC) against dermatologists. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) rather than continuous values.

datasets_used.name: Dermoscopic image dataset (referenced from Haenssle et al. 2018)
datasets_used.description: The dataset used in the original study by Haenssle et al. (Ann Oncol 2018; 29:1836–1842) for dermoscopic melanoma recognition, which is the subject of this correspondence. The specific name, source, or size of this dataset is not provided in the text.

use.validation: Used to select the operating point for the AI system (likely post-experiment based on the critique).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, ROC area) of the CNN and compare it to 58 dermatologists.

statistical_methods_used.method: ROC-AUC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the diagnostic performance of the deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) against dermatologists.

statistical_methods_used.method: ROC area (average of sensitivity and specificity at a given operating point)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the diagnostic performance of the CNN and dermatologists at specific operating points, as calculated by the authors in response to the critique.
To report diagnostic accuracy at specific operating points (e.g., matching human sensitivity levels) for both the CNN and dermatologists.

statistical_methods_used.method: Alternative AUC estimation methods (van den Hout; Katostaras and Katostara)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the AUC of ROC curves when only a single operating point is available, as suggested in the reply to address methodological concerns.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper discusses diagnostic performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, ROC-AUC) and methodological comparisons without reporting p-values or significance tests.

Haenssle et al. 2018 Study (Dermoscopic.notes: Oakden-Rayner calculated the ROC area for the CNN at the specified operating point as 79, noting no difference between the model and dermatologists in these experiments. Haenssle et al. clarified that the CNN's ROC-AUC (0.864) and a dermatologist's ROC area (0.863) are almost identical. Oakden-Rayner noted that the ROC area for the model was not presented in the original article. Haenssle et al. stated that alternative assessments of AUC using methods by van den Hout or Katostaras and Katostara yield similar results.

conclusions: The diagnostic performance of the deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) is comparable to that of expert dermatologists.
The CNN's ROC-AUC (0.864) and the dermatologist's calculated ROC area (0.863) are almost identical, validating the performance comparison despite metric differences.
While comparing the CNN's ROC-AUC with the average ROC area of dermatologists is not ideal, it represents one of the available options for assessment.
Alternative methods for estimating the AUC of ROC curves from a single operating point (such as those by van den Hout or Katostaras and Katostara) yield similar results to the reported values.
important_remarks: The original study by Haenssle et al. was critiqued for methodological issues, specifically the inappropriate comparison of the CNN's ROC-AUC with the dermatologists' 'ROC area' (average of sensitivity and specificity at a single operating point), which may have overestimated AI performance and underestimated human performance.

The selection of the CNN's operating point to exactly match human sensitivity levels was likely performed post-experiment, raising concerns about data leakage or selection bias that could artificially inflate the reported diagnostic accuracy.

The steepness of the ROC curve in the region of interest suggests that small changes in the operating point could lead to large reductions in sensitivity or specificity, indicating that the reported performance estimates may be unstable or significantly overestimated.
The original article did not present the ROC area for the CNN model, making independent verification of the comparison with dermatologists difficult.

Haenssle et al. clarified that the CNN's ROC-AUC (0.864) and a representative dermatologist's ROC area (0.863) are nearly identical, arguing that while the metric comparison is not ideal, it is a valid option given the constraints of comparing single operating points.
